story: Two AI systems now watch Cook Inlet's endangered belugas: a robot ear (deep-learning passive acoustics) and a robot eye (GAIA satellite computer vision). Both fresh June 2026, NOAA-led. Honest turn: watching is not saving.

on_screen_safe.status: Endangered under the Endangered Species Act (Cook Inlet DPS)
on_screen_safe.gaia: GAIA = Geospatial Artificial Intelligence For Animals; machine learning / computer vision on very-high-resolution satellite imagery; NOAA page updated 06/25/2026
on_screen_safe.gaia_partners: NOAA Fisheries, U.S. Geological Survey, Microsoft AI for Good, Naval Research Laboratory
on_screen_safe.gaia_areas: 5 areas across Cook Inlet: upper inlet (Susitna Delta, Chickaloon Bay, Turnagain and Knik Arms), Trading Bay, Redoubt Bay, Kenai, Tuxedni Bay
on_screen_safe.acoustic_paper: Adaptive Acoustic Monitoring for Endangered Cook Inlet Beluga Whales in Complex Soundscapes, Marine Mammal Science, online June 7 2026, DOI 10.1111/mms.70213
on_screen_safe.acoustic_method: open-source deep learning framework for passive acoustic monitoring; dual-stage model that separates signal detection from species classification
on_screen_safe.acoustic_team: Manuel Castellote (NOAA Fisheries) + Microsoft AI for Good (Dodhia, Miao, Lavista Ferres)
on_screen_safe.honest_limit: AI improves monitoring; it does not by itself reverse the decline. NOAA attributes the endangered status to ocean noise, prey limitations, habitat impacts, contaminants, predation, and strandings.
